file_verdicts.summary: Top-level curl in PKGBUILD risks supply-chain attack

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/chromium-snapshot-bin.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-ku3iqkh_...
Cloned repository to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-ku3iqkh_
Analyzing chromium-snapshot-bin AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-ku3iqkh_) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>UNSAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains a top-level command substitution using `curl` to fetch the latest Chromium build number from an external URL:

```
_build=$(curl -s "https://commondatastorage.googleapis.com/chromium-browser-snapshots/Linux_x64/LAST_CHANGE")
```

This command runs during the sourcing of the PKGBUILD, which occurs when `makepkg` is invoked—even with `--nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. The result is that simply parsing the PKGBUILD triggers an untrusted network request to download data from Google's storage bucket. While the destination (`commondatastorage.googleapis.com`) is a legitimate Google Cloud Storage endpoint used by the official Chromium project, **the act of performing an uncontrolled network call during PKGBUILD parse time is itself a supply-chain risk**.

More critically, the value of `_build` is later used to construct source URLs for downloading binary blobs (the Chrome and Chromedriver ZIP files). Although those downloads happen later during the actual `makepkg` run (and would be validated by checksums), the initial `curl` call **runs unconditionally and its output influences which binaries are fetched**, without any integrity checks on the `_build` value itself. This creates a potential window for an attacker to poison the `LAST_CHANGE` file (via compromise of the storage bucket or MITM, though HTTPS mitigates the latter) and thereby force the download of a malicious build.

While the use of `curl` here serves a functional purpose (determining the latest snapshot), **executing network requests in the top-level scope of a PKGBUILD is dangerous and can be abused in supply-chain attacks**. This should be replaced with a static `pkgver()` function or a safer mechanism (e.g., using `source` for version metadata). As written, merely sourcing the PKGBUILD can trigger external, unguarded behavior that affects build inputs.
</details>
<summary>
Top-level curl in PKGBUILD risks supply-chain attack
</summary>
</security_assessment>

x Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Top-level curl in PKGBUILD risks supply-chain attack[/]

Audit complete! Result: Unsafe -- DO NOT INSTALL!
# Issues (1 total)

## PKGBUILD

Status: UNSAFE

Summary: Top-level curl in PKGBUILD risks supply-chain attack

Details:

The PKGBUILD contains a top-level command substitution using `curl` to fetch the latest Chromium build number from an external URL:

```
_build=$(curl -s "https://commondatastorage.googleapis.com/chromium-browser-snapshots/Linux_x64/LAST_CHANGE")
```

This command runs during the sourcing of the PKGBUILD, which occurs when `makepkg` is invoked—even with `--nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. The result is that simply parsing the PKGBUILD triggers an untrusted network request to download data from Google's storage bucket. While the destination (`commondatastorage.googleapis.com`) is a legitimate Google Cloud Storage endpoint used by the official Chromium project, **the act of performing an uncontrolled network call during PKGBUILD parse time is itself a supply-chain risk**.

More critically, the value of `_build` is later used to construct source URLs for downloading binary blobs (the Chrome and Chromedriver ZIP files). Although those downloads happen later during the actual `makepkg` run (and would be validated by checksums), the initial `curl` call **runs unconditionally and its output influences which binaries are fetched**, without any integrity checks on the `_build` value itself. This creates a potential window for an attacker to poison the `LAST_CHANGE` file (via compromise of the storage bucket or MITM, though HTTPS mitigates the latter) and thereby force the download of a malicious build.

While the use of `curl` here serves a functional purpose (determining the latest snapshot), **executing network requests in the top-level scope of a PKGBUILD is dangerous and can be abused in supply-chain attacks**. This should be replaced with a static `pkgver()` function or a safer mechanism (e.g., using `source` for version metadata). As written, merely sourcing the PKGBUILD can trigger external, unguarded behavior that affects build inputs.

---

Analyzing chromium-snapshot-bin AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-3_1asad6) with deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD's global scope sets `_build` via a `curl` to Google's official API (`commondatastorage.googleapis.com`), which is standard for Chromium snapshot packages and does not execute arbitrary code. The `source` array downloads from the same trusted upstream and includes proper checksums for static files. The `pkgver()` function runs `chrome-linux/chrome --version`, which executes the upstream binary after extraction. While executing a binary from an untrusted source carries inherent supply-chain risk, this is the official upstream application; the PKGBUILD itself does not inject any malicious code or deviate from normal packaging practices. No obfuscation, exfiltration, or unexpected network destinations are present. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will only fetch sources and execute the above, which is safe given the scope of this gate.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR snapshot PKGBUILD with no injected malicious code.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Reviewed LICENSE. Status: SAFE -- Standard license file, no security concerns.
LLM auditresponse for chromium-snapshot-bin.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a standard `.install` script that only prints informational messages to the user during package installation or upgrade. It defines colored output helpers (`msg_blue`, `note`) and a `post_install` function that outputs notes about Pepper Flash auto-detection, the location of custom flags, and the launcher name. There are no network requests, no dangerous commands (curl, wget, eval, base64), no file modifications, no data exfiltration, and no hidden or obfuscated code. The script is completely benign and conforms to normal AUR packaging practices.
</details>
<summary>Non-malicious install script with only user notes.</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/3] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed chromium-snapshot-bin.install. Status: SAFE -- Non-malicious install script with only user notes.
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD follows standard AUR packaging practices for a Chromium snapshot binary package. It fetches the latest build number from Google's official Chromium snapshot server (`commondatastorage.googleapis.com`) and downloads the corresponding `chrome-linux.zip` and `chromedriver_linux64.zip` archives. The `sha1sums` for those archives are set to `SKIP`, which is expected for mutable snapshot sources and not an indicator of malice. All other files (scripts, icons, desktop entry, license) have verified checksums.

The `package()` function performs routine installation steps: creating directories, setting permissions (including `chmod 4755` on the sandbox binary, which is required by Chromium), placing binaries, icons, a desktop file, a man page, and the license. There are no suspicious network requests to untrusted hosts, no obfuscated commands, no eval/base64 tricks, and no attempts to exfiltrate or tamper with system data. The sole external network interaction is with the official Chromium build server, which is the package's declared upstream. While the unpinned source and dynamic version fetch introduce a supply-chain risk in principle, they are typical for snapshot packages and do not constitute malicious or dangerous behavior by the AUR maintainer.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR PKGBUILD for Chromium snapshot; no malicious behavior found.</summary>
</security_assessment>

There is no executable code, no obfuscation, and no indication of malicious activity. The use of HTML entity encoding for quotes is simply a formatting choice and does not hide any dangerous content. This file is part of the Chromium browser's built-in component and is harmless.
</details>

This file is a standard Chrome extension content script. This is normal behavior for injecting extension scripts into web pages. There is no obfuscation, no external network requests, no system file operations, and no deviation from standard Chromium extension practices. The code is consistent with the upstream Chromium project and serves the legitimate purpose of enabling reading mode in Google Docs.
</details>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file is a standard Google Docs Canvas annotation script included with Chromium. It sets a window variable (`_docs_annotate_canvas_by_ext`) to the extension ID, enabling Google Docs accessibility features. The use of `eval` is mitigated by a Trusted Types policy, which is a security best practice. No suspicious behavior, external requests, or obfuscation is present. It serves the stated purpose of enabling a11y annotations for Google Docs when running as a Chrome extension.
</details>
<summary>Legitimate Chromium extension script for Docs a11y.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for chromium-snapshot-bin.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This is a standard wrapper script for launching the Chromium snapshot binary. It reads optional user flags from `~/.config/chromium-flags.conf`, detects Pepper Flash if installed, sets environment variables (including public Google API keys that are routinely included in Arch Chromium packages), and then executes the main binary. There are no network requests, no obfuscated code, and no unexpected file operations. All commands (`grep`, `cut`, `readlink`, `exec`) serve legitimate packaging purposes. The script does not download or execute any code from untrusted sources, nor does it exfiltrate data. It is entirely consistent with normal AUR packaging practices.
</details>
<summary>Standard Chromium wrapper, no malicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<details>
This file is the standard Chromium wrapper script (`chrome-wrapper`) from the upstream Chromium source. It performs normal desktop integration tasks: checking for an existing desktop file, generating one if missing, setting up the library path, and creating symlinks for missing libraries (e.g., libbz2.so.1.0, NSS libraries) from the system into the application directory. All file operations are limited to the user's local `.local/share/applications` directory and the Chromium installation directory. There are no network requests, no obfuscated code, no execution of untrusted content, and no data exfiltration. The script is entirely consistent with expected Chromium packaging and does not exhibit any signs of a supply-chain attack.
</details>
<summary>Standard upstream Chromium wrapper; no malicious behavior found.</summary>
</security_assessment>

Final Status: SAFE


No issues found.

data.reasoning: The qwen model flagged the PKGBUILD as unsafe solely because of a top-level `curl` to commondatastorage.googleapis.com used to fetch the latest Chromium snapshot build number. This is a well-established, legitimate pattern for snapshot/VCS-style AUR packages. The endpoint is Google's official Chromium snapshot storage over HTTPS, the resulting value only constructs download URLs (which are then handled by makepkg's source mechanism), and there is no code execution, obfuscation, or exfiltration. The deepseek model reviewed all 13 relevant files including PKGBUILD, the .install script, both wrapper scripts, and the desktop entry — finding nothing malicious. The qwen verdict is a false positive driven by an overly conservative heuristic against any top-level network call. There is no actual maintainer-introduced malicious behavior; the package merely tracks an upstream binary, which carries the normal supply-chain trust assumption inherent to all -bin packages, not a specific vulnerability.
data.disagreement_analysis: The disagreement stems entirely from interpretation of the top-level `curl` in PKGBUILD. qwen treated 'network call at PKGBUILD parse time' as inherently dangerous and stopped its audit immediately (only 1 file reviewed, 429 completion tokens). deepseek correctly contextualized it as standard practice for Chromium snapshot packaging and proceeded with a full 13-file review. Note the two reports also audited slightly different pkgvers (151.0.7901 vs 151.0.7872), but this does not affect the verdict since both reference the same PKGBUILD logic.
coverage_issues: qwen aborted after reviewing only the PKGBUILD due to its early-exit on the curl finding, so it never examined the .install script, wrapper scripts, or desktop file — though in this case the PKGBUILD was correctly the gating file and contained no actual threat.
false_positives: qwen: 'Top-level curl risks supply-chain attack' — fetching a build number from Google's official HTTPS Chromium snapshot bucket is the standard, expected mechanism for this class of package; the value only parameterizes source URLs and introduces no code execution or unverified payload beyond the inherent trust of any -bin package.
learnings: The audit tool should treat top-level `curl`/`wget` that merely fetches a version/build identifier from a known-trusted upstream over HTTPS (used to construct source URLs) as a LOW-severity informational note, not an automatic UNSAFE verdict — especially for snapshot/-bin packages where this is idiomatic.

An early-exit on the initial PKGBUILD gate should distinguish 'arbitrary code execution / data exfiltration' (true blocker) from 'benign network metadata fetch' (proceed with audit). qwen's gate logic is too coarse and prevented full coverage.

Provide models with reference context that for -bin packages tracking upstream binaries, the 'supply-chain risk' is the upstream trust assumption itself, which is expected and not maintainer-introduced malice; reports should not escalate this to UNSAFE without evidence of tampering or a non-official endpoint.
Consider normalizing the pkgver/snapshot across model runs so disagreements reflect genuine analysis differences rather than transient upstream version differences.
